name: Big Cedar Lodge
tagline: Six courses, top-100 pedigree, and Ozark views that span for miles. Johnny Morris built a golfer's playground in the hills above Branson — and we're still not sure anywhere else in the country stacks up.
meta_description: Big Cedar Lodge golf trip guide — six Ozark courses including Payne's Valley, Buffalo Ridge, Ozarks National, and the new Cliffhangers par 3.
the_take: Big Cedar has one of the best collections of top-tier courses in the country. Johnny Morris built six of them in the Ozarks, and three of those (Payne's Valley, Buffalo Ridge, and Ozarks National) sit on Golf Digest's top-100 list. The elevation makes it feel different from anywhere else. Every course plays through the hills, and the views from the tee boxes stretch for miles.

Everyone shows up for Payne's Valley because that's the Tiger course. The surprise is that Buffalo Ridge and Ozarks National are right there with it. We walked off Payne's thinking we'd just played the best course on property, then teed up at Buffalo Ridge the next day and had to reconsider.
It feels like you showed up for a hunting trip and somebody handed you tee times on six top-tier courses instead.

courses.name: Buffalo Ridge
courses.description: The first course built on the property and one of the most underrated stops in the country. The views start on the very first tee box and never let up. Conditioning was as good as anywhere we've played, and the bison-skull tee markers at the tips are the kind of detail that sticks with you. Tough but playable, with water features pulling the Ozark landscape into the routing. Must-play.

courses.name: Payne's Valley
courses.description: The crew favorite. Wide off the tee and very playable, with the kind of risk-reward layout that keeps a buddies trip honest. Stop at the exclusive pro shop after the front nine for gear you can't get anywhere else. Then play the 19th — an island green tucked into a rock amphitheater with a waterfall behind it, money on the line for a hole-in-one. (Fun fact: Johnny Morris designed the 19th himself.) Must-play.

courses.name: Ozarks National
courses.description: Fast and firm, exactly what you'd expect from Coore & Crenshaw if you've played their other stuff. A lot of people on property will tell you this is the best course at Big Cedar. We don't agree, but it's still worth a round. Play it once.

courses.name: Cliffhangers
courses.description: The newest course on property. An 18-hole par 3 carved into the limestone cliffs above the 19th hole at Payne's, with waterfalls and a cave system routed directly into the experience. Brand new and almost certainly going to climb every "best par 3 course in America" list within a year.

courses.name: Top of the Rock
courses.description: Down the road from the main Payne's pro shop, with arguably the best views Big Cedar has to offer. A sunset round here is the move. Must-play par 3.

courses.name: Mountain Top
courses.description: A 13-hole walking-only par 3 that climbs up and around the Payne's Valley pro shop. Easy to skip when your itinerary's full. Don't. The views are some of the best on property and the walk is worth every step.

where_to_stay: We stayed at Angler's Lodge, a few minutes down the road from the main resort — easy access to all the courses and a noticeable step down in price from the on-property options. For a buddies trip on a normal budget, it was the right call. If you've got the budget, stay in one of the lodges right off Payne's Valley. They're bigger, more spacious, and you can practically roll out of bed into the pro shop. Couples should book a room inside the main Big Cedar Lodge.
booking_text: Book a stay at Big Cedar Lodge →

setup.label: 2 DAYS
setup.itinerary: Payne's Valley one day, Buffalo Ridge the other. Squeeze in Cliffhangers and Top of the Rock around the main rounds — before tee time or after the back nine. Lunch at the main clubhouse on one of the days.

setup.label: 3 DAYS
setup.itinerary: Hit Payne's, Buffalo, and Ozarks once each. If you're a golf sicko, make one of the days a 36-hole day. Otherwise pair each main round with one of the par 3 courses (Mountain Top, Top of the Rock, or Cliffhangers).

setup.label: 4+ DAYS
setup.itinerary: Same as the 3-day plan with breathing room, or play Payne's and Buffalo Ridge twice. Both hold up to a second look. Time Top of the Rock for golden hour.

know_before.label: BEST TIME
know_before.value: Late April through May, or September into October. The Ozarks get hot and sticky in summer and cold in winter — shoulder season is the sweet spot.

know_before.label: FLY INTO
know_before.value: Springfield-Branson (SGF), about 45 minutes from the resort. Bentonville (XNA) is a workable backup if you can't get into SGF.

know_before.label: BUDGET
know_before.value: Lodging runs $350–$1,500 per person per night depending on where you sleep. Golf: $250–$300 at Buffalo Ridge or Ozarks National, $400–$500 at Payne's Valley, $100–$150 at the par 3 courses. Food and snacks on the course are included with your tee time.

know_before.label: CART vs WALKING
know_before.value: Mountain Top is walking-only. Everything else you can ride.

know_before.label: TEE TIMES
know_before.value: Resort guests can book well in advance. The public window is only 30 days out, which makes a same-trip slot a coin flip. Stay on-site and lock them early.

what_to_skip.lead: A second round at Ozarks National.
what_to_skip.detail: A lot of people there will swear it's the best course on property. We don't agree. Play it once, then put that next round into Payne's or Buffalo Ridge.

what_to_skip.lead: Loading up on Payne's Valley merch.
what_to_skip.detail: The logo doesn't hit. Save your money for the pro shops at Buffalo Ridge and Ozarks National — better gear, better logos, better stories when somebody asks where you got it.

name: Gamble Sands
tagline: Two David McLay Kidd courses above the Columbia River. Hard to get to, easy to fall for. Brewster might be the most underrated stop in American golf.
meta_description: Gamble Sands golf trip guide — two David McLay Kidd courses above the Columbia River in Brewster, Washington, including the 2025 Golf Digest Best New Public Course.
the_take: Gamble Sands is what happens when somebody decides to build world-class links golf on the high plains of central Washington and doesn't compromise. The original course opened in 2014 and has been climbing the rankings ever since. Then in 2025 they opened Scarecrow, a second David McLay Kidd design that picked up Golf Digest's Best New Public Course of the year. Two courses, same designer, same terrain, completely different rounds. The views over the Columbia River are the kind of thing you assume only exist in drone shots until you stand on a tee box and see it for yourself. There are also a few holes where you'll catch a look at the vineyards sitting on the slopes below the property.

Scarecrow. They opened it on the same property as the original, with the same designer, on the same kind of terrain. It should have felt like a copy. It doesn't. Walking off both courses we honestly couldn't pick a favorite.
Two of the best public courses in the country, sitting on a stretch of the Columbia River nobody else thought to build on.

courses.name: Gamble Sands
courses.description: The original. Wide, rolling fairways that play firm and fast, with the Columbia River sitting below the property like a backdrop somebody painted in. Plays harder than it looks because the contours run in every direction. A little more forgiving off the tee than Scarecrow. Must-play.

courses.name: Scarecrow
courses.description: Named Golf Digest's Best New Public Course of 2025 and it earned every word of that. Sits on the same property as the original but plays completely differently — new routing, new contours, and a few risk-reward holes that'll make you wish you had a second ball in your pocket. Slightly better views for most of the round in our opinion. Must-play. If you only have time for one, flip a coin. You can't lose.

where_to_stay: We stayed on-site at Gamble Sands — no question, this is the move. The rooms look out over the Columbia River with the putting green sitting below the deck. The lodge sits up a hill from the clubhouse, so you'll either drive down or grab the shuttle (a guy in a golf cart who runs people back and forth). Room type doesn't matter much — king rooms or doubles with two queens, most of them look out over the river either way. Nothing over the top, just nice and comfortable, exactly what you want after a day of golf. No reason to look at anything off-property.

setup.label: 2 DAYS
setup.itinerary: Play one main course each day and pair each round with QuickSands, the on-property short course, in the afternoon. Best way to keep the short game sharp between rounds.

setup.label: 3 DAYS
setup.itinerary: Both main courses plus QuickSands. The original Gamble Sands is a little more forgiving off the tee. Scarecrow has slightly better views for most of the round. Play them both and pick your favorite.

setup.label: 4+ DAYS
setup.itinerary: Two rounds on each course, multiple loops at QuickSands, plus a Coeur d'Alene or Spokane stop on the front or back end. Get out for a sunset round or at least a sunset beer on the deck — the Columbia River light at the end of a summer day is the part you'll remember.

know_before.label: BEST TIME
know_before.value: May through September is peak season for a reason. The eastern Washington summer is dry and hot, the golf is in great shape, and the long daylight lets you ride out into the evening. October is the sleeper move if you want lower lodging prices and don't mind cooler rounds.

know_before.label: FLY INTO
know_before.value: Spokane (GEG) is the closest airport with real flight options. About a two-hour drive to Brewster. Use it as an excuse to add Coeur d'Alene Resort to the itinerary on the way in or out.

know_before.label: BUDGET
know_before.value: Lodging swings hard with the season. Peak (May through September) runs around $600 per night. October drops as low as $150 per night for the same room. Greens fees run $300 per round in peak season, $200 off-peak.

know_before.label: WALKING vs CART
know_before.value: Cart-based golf. We rode both rounds. The terrain is gentle enough that you could walk if you wanted to, but the carts are part of how everyone moves around the property.

know_before.label: WEATHER
know_before.value: Eastern Washington is high desert. Dry, hot in summer, and the wind can show up unannounced. It does rain occasionally — we caught some on our trip — but nowhere near what Seattle or Portland get. Sunscreen and a wind shirt aren't optional in peak season.

what_to_skip.lead: Danny Boy's for a full sit-down meal.
what_to_skip.detail: It's fine, but we may have ordered wrong. The move is the Tower of Tots appetizer — best item on the menu. The Barn is the real food win on property. Don't sleep on it.
There's not much else to do here, and trying to make Brewster work as a couples weekend or family trip is going to leave somebody bored. This one's for the golf people.

what_to_skip.lead: Treating this as a quick one-stop trip.
what_to_skip.detail: Brewster isn't easy to get to. Spokane is still about two hours away. Build in time, pair it with Coeur d'Alene or Spokane on the front end, and you'll get a lot more out of the journey.

name: Palm Springs
tagline: More world-class desert golf than you can play in a week. November weather, pristine conditioning, and Pete Dye fingerprints all over the Coachella Valley.
meta_description: Palm Springs golf trip guide — four Coachella Valley courses including PGA West Stadium, Desert Willow, Escena, and the Westin Rancho Mirage Dye Course.
the_take: Palm Springs and the surrounding Coachella Valley have more good public courses than any single golf destination we've been to. The desert weather keeps the courses in pristine condition almost year-round, and the variety is the part that surprised us. Pete Dye stadium-style tracks, mountain-view layouts, Nicklaus designs, resort courses, all within a 30-minute drive of each other. November through April is peak season for a reason.

Two things. First, the number of top-tier courses crammed into the Coachella Valley is wild. You can fly out for a week and never run out of options. Second, PGA West Stadium plays way more fair in person than it looks on TV during the AMEX. We were braced for a beating and got a fun round instead.
A buddies trip golf factory. Show up in November, pick from a dozen great courses, and never wait on a tee box.

courses.name: PGA West — Pete Dye Stadium
courses.description: The famous one. Host of the AMEX on the PGA Tour. We expected to get beat up after watching pros struggle here on TV, but it played surprisingly fair for mid-handicappers. Forced carries and big bunkers all over the property, but the lines are honest. The 17th — the Alcatraz island green — is the one you came for. Must-play.

courses.name: Desert Willow — Mountain View
courses.description: One of the two courses at Desert Willow and the one most people queue up for first. Conditioning was excellent and the layout makes you think on almost every shot. Less famous than PGA West but holds its own. Worth playing.

courses.name: Escena Golf Club
courses.description: Sits right next to the Palm Springs airport. Normally we'd write off a course this close to a runway, but somehow it wasn't distracting. The big jetliners coming in low against the mountain backdrop was actually a cool visual. Clean modern feel, views across the valley, layout that rewards smart play. Solid public option.

courses.name: Westin Rancho Mirage
courses.description: Right on property at the Westin, which made it the most convenient round of the trip. Classic Pete Dye routing that tries to mess with your head and tricky greens that follow through on the threat. Easy to add to your itinerary if you're staying at the Westin — which we recommend.

where_to_stay: We stayed at the Westin Rancho Mirage — right on property of the Dye Course, which made one of the four rounds a walk from the lobby. Solid lodging with nothing to complain about. The amenities are big: pool with waterslides, pickleball courts, tennis, spa. Good food on property plus plenty of options outside the resort if you want to leave for dinner. Standard rooms were more than enough. If you're pairing it with a couples trip, look at rooms with patio access.

setup.label: 2 DAYS
setup.itinerary: PGA West Stadium one day, then either Desert Willow Mountain View or Escena the next. You can't go wrong either way.

setup.label: 3 DAYS
setup.itinerary: Make one of the days a 36-hole day and get all four courses in. Most efficient way to do it.

setup.label: 4+ DAYS
setup.itinerary: Spread it out. Play all four we played and start working through the rest of the Coachella Valley list. Don't skip PGA West Stadium because of the price tag — you'll be happy you kept it in the itinerary. The number of good courses out here is the whole point.

know_before.label: BEST TIME
know_before.value: November through April is peak for a reason. Perfect weather, courses are in their best shape. The rest of the year gets brutally hot. If you can stand the heat, summer and shoulder months will get you on the same courses for a fraction of the cost.

know_before.label: FLY INTO
know_before.value: Palm Springs International (PSP) is right there, about 15 minutes to most of the courses. The catch is that PSP is small, so flight options can be limited. Ontario (ONT) or LAX are backups, but you're looking at a 90+ minute drive from either.

know_before.label: BUDGET
know_before.value: The bundle is the move. Tee Times USA hosts a package with the four courses we played plus lodging at the Westin Rancho Mirage, starting at $837 per person. Outside the package, expect $200–$400+ per round on the headline tracks and $250–$500 per night for the resort in peak season.

know_before.label: WALKING vs CART
know_before.value: Cart-based golf. Desert courses don't lend themselves to walking. Don't sweat it.

know_before.label: WEATHER
know_before.value: The desert heat is real, even outside summer. Sunscreen, plenty of water, and an extra shirt aren't optional in midday rounds. The conditioning is the best part — every course we played was in great shape.

what_to_skip.lead: Flying into PSP at any cost.
what_to_skip.detail: The airport is convenient but small, which means flights can get pricey or awkward. Compare PSP, ONT, and LAX and pick the one that makes sense for your itinerary.

what_to_skip.lead: Booking a Palm Springs trip in July.
what_to_skip.detail: The heat will undo any golf gains you came for. If you need off-season pricing, shoot for late October or early April instead of dead summer.

what_to_skip.lead: Showing up at PGA West Stadium expecting to get beat up.
what_to_skip.detail: Watching the pros struggle there during the AMEX makes the course look gnarly, but it plays way more fair than that on a normal tee. Pick a tee that matches your game and you'll have a great round.

name: SentryWorld
tagline: The best-conditioned course we've ever played, sitting quietly in central Wisconsin.
meta_description: SentryWorld golf trip guide — Robert Trent Jones Jr.'s masterpiece in Stevens Point, Wisconsin.
the_take: SentryWorld is Sentry Insurance's golf resort in Stevens Point. Robert Trent Jones Jr. designed it in the 80s, they redid the course in 2014, and the Inn at SentryWorld opened in 2022. You'll wonder how an insurance company ended up running one of the best resort experiences in the country. Spend a weekend there and you stop asking.

The green fee covers unlimited food and drinks. Not just water and bananas. Full meals, hot food, snacks, beer, cocktails. Two refreshment stations stocked like a hotel mini-bar that you pass multiple times during the round. $375 sounds steep until you realize lunch and a few drinks are baked in.

Tucked away, premium, never stuffy. The Inn feels like a really nice friend's renovated cabin. The course is the most pristine thing we've ever seen. When we put the drone up before our first round, we counted crews on basically every hole prepping for the first tee time. You leave feeling like you got the full Pebble or Pinehurst treatment for half the trip cost.

courses.name: SentryWorld
courses.description: The 16th is the famous Flowers Hole — 50,000+ flowers surrounding a par 3 — and it lives up to the hype. But the conditioning is what sets this place apart. Bunkers raked tight, fairways like carpet, greens true and quick. Worth playing twice on any trip: first round to figure out where you're going, second round to actually play it.

where_to_stay: We stayed at the Inn at SentryWorld both years — no question, this is the right call. It's a five-minute walk to the first tee and the rooms feel like a beautifully renovated cabin. Staying off property means driving in from Stevens Point or Wausau and missing the whole point. If you stay on property there is no need to leave the entire time. They have amazing food at PJ's (the restaurant on site) and the breakfast spot right off the lobby of the Inn. King rooms are big, bathrooms are nice, fire pit out back.

setup.label: 2 DAYS
setup.itinerary: Two rounds at SentryWorld, one night at the Inn. Arrive Friday afternoon, play that day, sleep, play again Saturday morning, drive out Sunday. Tight but it works.

setup.label: 3 DAYS
setup.itinerary: Three rounds at SentryWorld over two nights. First round to learn it, second to score, third just because you'll want to. By round three you'll know exactly which refreshment station has your favorite snacks.

setup.label: 4+ DAYS
setup.itinerary: Make it a Wisconsin trip. Two days at SentryWorld, then drive an hour west to Sand Valley for two more. The resorts don't have a partnership but they pair well. SentryWorld for the pristine resort feel, Sand Valley for rugged sand-country golf.

know_before.label: BEST TIME
know_before.value: July

know_before.label: FLY INTO
know_before.value: Central Wisconsin (CWA) in Mosinee is closest, about 25 minutes away. If you can't get there direct, Milwaukee (MKE) is around 2.5 hours and Madison (MSN) is about 2 hours.

know_before.label: BUDGET
know_before.value: $375 a round, includes unlimited food and drinks on course. A 2-night/2-round stay-and-play at the Inn runs about $1,000 per person. Not cheap, but you're not paying for food on top.

know_before.label: FOOD
know_before.value: Do not skip eating at least two meals at PJ's — the food is excellent. The cheese curds are a must. The breakfast spot right off the Inn lobby is the right move before a morning round.

know_before.label: WEATHER
know_before.value: Wisconsin in July sits mid-70s to mid-80s, mostly sunny. Light layer for early tee times. Rain can roll through but doesn't usually camp out.

what_to_skip.detail: We've seen guys skip them and miss the point. The food alone is worth $50 of your green fee. They're stocked enough to be your lunch — don't book a big clubhouse meal afterward expecting to be hungry.

what_to_skip.lead: Trying to score your first round.
what_to_skip.detail: You won't. The course is too tricky off the tee to figure out cold and you'll be too busy admiring how pure this place is. Just enjoy the first round and the atmosphere. Second round is for actually playing.

what_to_skip.lead: Driving in for a single round.
what_to_skip.detail: Don't do it. Plan for two rounds minimum. SentryWorld stands on its own — two rounds and the Inn is a full weekend, no add-ons needed.

name: St. George
tagline: Red rock. Black lava. Zero bad holes.
meta_description: St. George Utah golf trip guide — the most visually dramatic golf in America, with red rock canyons and ancient lava fields.

name: Tucson
tagline: Desert golf at The Kennedy Hotel.
meta_description: Tucson golf trip guide — 4 nights and 4 rounds all-in at $697 per person, staying at The Kennedy Hotel.

name: Dominican Republic
tagline: Two resorts, four courses, and ocean holes that ruin you for anything back home. The DR sneaks up on you.
meta_description: Dominican Republic golf trip guide — Teeth of the Dog, Dye Fore, Corales, and La Cana across Casa de Campo and Puntacana.
the_take: The DR punches way above its weight in golf. Pete Dye's Teeth of the Dog at Casa de Campo has been on every "best courses in the world" list for 50-plus years. Tom Fazio's Corales at Puntacana hosts a PGA Tour event with a finishing stretch (the Devil's Elbow) that rivals anything in the Caribbean. P.B. Dye's La Cana mixes inland and ocean holes across 27 holes. And then there's Dye Fore, sitting up on the cliffs above the Chavon River — which nobody talks about until they play it.

Dye Fore is the surprise. Nobody told us about this course before we booked. We figured Teeth of the Dog was the trip and the rest were bonus rounds. Then we played the Chavon 9 at Dye Fore — 300 feet above the Chavon River, mountains on one side, Caribbean off in the distance — and it ended up being the biggest change of pace on the trip. Think Big Cedar Lodge, but in the DR.
Caribbean ocean golf with enough variety that you're not playing the same view four days in a row.

courses.name: Teeth of the Dog
courses.description: The reason you book this trip. Seven holes hug the ocean and the rest of the course earns its spot in the conversation. Pete Dye famously said he built 11 holes and God built the other seven, and he wasn't wrong. Just came out of a full restoration — re-grassed with Dynasty Paspalum, new sand on the fairways — so it's playing as good as it's looked in years. Tee one up at the island green on the back nine. Forecaddies mandatory. Must-play, and the best course on the trip.

courses.name: Corales
courses.description: PGA Tour stop and one of the best ocean stretches we've ever played. The Devil's Elbow (holes 16, 17, and 18) is the kind of finish that makes you forget what you shot. Mandatory caddies — sounds like a tax until you're standing over a 6-iron with the Caribbean breeze pushing your ball toward a cliff. Trust them. Must-play.

courses.name: Dye Fore
courses.description: The sleeper. Don't skip it. The Chavon 9 plays 300 feet above the river with mountain views on one side and Caribbean glimpses off in the distance. It's basically Big Cedar in the DR. Forecaddies mandatory here too. Must-play.

courses.name: La Cana
courses.description: Three nines — we played Tortuga and Arrecife. Both have ocean holes that hold their own against anything else on the trip. Conditioning wasn't quite at the level of Corales or Teeth, but we got the timing right on the finishing hole and watched the sun set over the Caribbean. Worth playing if you're at Puntacana.

where_to_stay: Splitting between two resorts is basically required. You have to be checked in as a guest to play Teeth of the Dog and Dye Fore — hard rule at Casa de Campo. Same setup at Puntacana for Corales and La Cana. We did two nights at the Westin Puntacana for the Corales and La Cana days, then one night at Casa de Campo for Teeth of the Dog and Dye Fore. The Westin is the smaller, more intimate property with beach access right off the lobby — Traditional Rooms are fine, upgrade to ocean-view if the budget allows. Casa de Campo is a 7,000-acre compound; you'll need a golf cart to get anywhere. Superior Casita Rooms were comfortable. Going bigger? The villas include breakfast and a touring cart.
booking_text: Book a stay at Casa de Campo →

setup.label: 2 DAYS
setup.itinerary: Stay at Casa de Campo. Teeth of the Dog one day, Dye Fore the next. Skip Puntacana if time's tight. The two Casa de Campo courses alone make the trip worth it.

setup.label: 3 DAYS
setup.itinerary: Add Corales. One night at the Westin for the Corales round, two at Casa de Campo for Teeth of the Dog and Dye Fore. La Cana becomes optional.

setup.label: 4+ DAYS
setup.itinerary: The full itinerary we played. Two nights at the Westin (Corales day, then La Cana with a sunset finish), then over to Casa de Campo for Teeth of the Dog and Dye Fore. Add a beach day at either resort if you've got the time.

know_before.label: BEST TIME
know_before.value: January through April. We went in early January and the weather was perfect. Hurricane season runs June through November — avoid it unless you like rolling the dice.

know_before.label: FLY INTO
know_before.value: Punta Cana International (PUJ). About 15 minutes from the Westin, about an hour from Casa de Campo. La Romana (LRM) is closer to Casa de Campo if you can find a flight.

know_before.label: BUDGET
know_before.value: Greens fees stack up fast: Teeth of the Dog and Corales are each in the $400+ range; Dye Fore and La Cana run $200–$300. Caddies and forecaddies add $50–$80 per round, cash, plus tip. Lodging is $300–$500 per night in season at both properties (villas higher). Plan on roughly $700–$1,000 per person per day all-in.

know_before.label: CADDIES
know_before.value: All four courses are cart-based with mandatory or strongly-encouraged caddies. Forecaddies on Teeth and Dye Fore. Caddies-with-carts at Corales (mandatory) and La Cana (optional for resort guests). Bring cash for tips on every round.

know_before.label: WEATHER
know_before.value: The Caribbean breeze is real, especially at Corales and Teeth of the Dog. Bring an extra glove (you'll sweat through the first one) and expect the wind to take ten yards off every shot on the ocean holes.

know_before.label: FOOD
know_before.value: The food at the Westin is excellent. Brassa (the steakhouse) and Bao (the Asian spot) are both worth a meal. Bao was our favorite.

what_to_skip.detail: You can, but you don't need to. Beach, food, and golf are all on property at both places. Save the excursions for a different kind of trip.

name: Evermore
tagline: Two Nicklaus courses, a resort built for families, and Disney down the street. Easily one of the best stay-and-plays in Orlando.
meta_description: Evermore Orlando golf trip guide — two Nicklaus Design courses, Evermore Bay, and Disney World fifteen minutes away.
the_take: Evermore is the rare stay-and-play that works for the whole family. Two Nicklaus Design courses sitting right next to a resort with amenities that hold up against anything in Orlando. Then there's Disney World just down the road, which makes it one of the only golf trips you can take where everyone in the family gets what they want out of it.

The resort itself. The pool has a massive waterslide and rope swings into Evermore Bay. Lodging scales from flats overlooking the water to full houses with private pools and golf carts to drive the property. And the golf still holds up against the best public tracks in the area. Easily one of the best stay-and-plays in Orlando.
A Florida resort that nailed the family golf trip. Golf in the morning, Evermore Bay in the afternoon, Disney World whenever you want it.

courses.name: Cypress Course
courses.description: Plays through the property's cypress hammocks and waterways. The standout is Hole 3 — an island green par 3 that gives you a little taste of Sawgrass 17. Conditioning was excellent both times we played, pace of play was solid, and the on-course food is a step up from what you usually get at a Florida resort. Must-play.

courses.name: Links Course
courses.description: More open than the Cypress, with the wind playing a bigger role. Pair it back-to-back with Cypress and you get genuine variety between the two rounds. Worth playing.

where_to_stay: We've done two trips here with two different setups. Year one: the flats overlooking Evermore Bay — spacious, full kitchen, living area, bunk room, three bedrooms, three full baths. Year two: one of the houses with a private pool and golf carts to drive the property. Both were great. The choice comes down to group size. Three to six people on a golf trip? The flats are more than enough. Bringing families? Get the house with the pool — the kids can run around without worrying about hotel hallways.

setup.label: 2 DAYS
setup.itinerary: Both courses back-to-back. Cypress one day, Links the other. Hang at Evermore Bay in the afternoons if you've brought family along.

setup.label: 3 DAYS
setup.itinerary: Play both courses, then circle back to whichever one you liked better for a third round. Use day two as the Disney World detour if the family's with you.

setup.label: 4+ DAYS
setup.itinerary: The full family-and-golf setup. Two rounds on each Evermore course, a Disney day, and a dedicated pool day at Evermore Bay. The waterslide and rope swings into the water are worth doing at least once — you're paying for the amenities either way.

know_before.label: BEST TIME
know_before.value: October through April. Florida summers are brutal and afternoon thunderstorms shut down golf almost daily from June through August. Shoulder months are the sweet spot.

know_before.label: FLY INTO
know_before.value: Orlando International (MCO) is about 25 minutes away. Easy in, easy out.

know_before.label: BUDGET
know_before.value: Each round at Evermore runs $200–$300 per person. Lodging swings widely — anywhere from $1,500 to $3,500 per night depending on whether you're in a flat or one of the bigger houses with a pool. Food on property is moderate to pricey. Plan on a chunky daily spend once you stack golf, lodging, and meals.

know_before.label: WALKING vs CART
know_before.value: Cart-based golf, nothing weird.

know_before.label: WEATHER
know_before.value: Florida summer storms are real. Check the radar before any round between June and August. Otherwise conditions are excellent year-round.

what_to_skip.detail: It's fine, but the food worth coming back for is over by the bay. That's where you want to eat.

what_to_skip.lead: Treating it as a golf-only trip.
what_to_skip.detail: The whole point of Evermore is that you can mix family stuff with golf. If Disney World is in your radius of interest, work it in. Treat it like a stay-and-play only and you're leaving half the value on the table.

what_to_skip.lead: Skipping the pool if you've got kids.
what_to_skip.detail: The waterslide and rope swings into Evermore Bay are the part everyone remembers. You're paying for the resort amenities whether you use them or not.
